directorPlan.scene_setting: A biology prep bench during a short lab-skills check, just before a simple food-web worksheet is copied for a class set.
directorPlan.scene_opening: A laminated arrow key, a pencil, and the learner's first food-web draft lie under the same metal clip.
directorPlan.relationship: A practical lab-skills coach checking a learner's diagram before it becomes the model sheet for others.
directorPlan.stakes: If the arrow rule stays wrong, the copied food web will teach the whole group that arrows mean pursuit instead of energy transfer.
directorPlan.locale: Modern standard English in a school lab-workshop register; concise, procedural, and artifact-led.
directorPlan.register: Low-warmth, concrete, unshowy; pressure comes from the worksheet deadline, not from emotional confrontation.
directorPlan.stage_direction_style_prompt: Use one concrete object action or material arrangement that changes what the speakers can honestly say.
directorPlan.voice_constraints: Begin from the marked worksheet or arrow key, not from reassurance. Avoid generic tutoring phrases such as "I hear you," "great question," or "your intuition is right." Use exact labels like grass, rabbit, hawk, food, eater, and energy. Keep speech modern and direct; no dialect performance, no ornate theatrical imitation. Pedagogical source: Make social assistance visible: a shared worksheet, tool, peer standard, or workplace procedure can hold part of the task. Dialogue source: Keep a concrete artifact in view. The object should force a distinction faster than a lecture would. Stage-direction source: Use one concrete object action or material arrangement that changes what the speakers can honestly say.
directorPlan.person_policy: First and second person are allowed but should not dominate. Prefer object references, diagram labels, and short procedural statements over repeated I/you framing.
directorPlan.direct_address_budget: No more than one direct you/your validation beat per public turn; validation should name a usable piece of the diagram rather than praise the person.

side_constraints.tutor: Keep the shared prefix as a routine notation repair: arrow from food to eater, one ordinary check, no theatrical reversal unless the branch pressure supplies it. Offer a scaffold that is narrow enough to use and temporary enough to remove. Mark what the learner can already do without making it a compliment. Begin from the object or measurement before abstraction. Peripeteia branch: when hidden learner pressure is supplied, the next tutor turn should visibly move from the prior route to a different mechanism-level route. Do not merely repeat the same object, example, graph, checklist, question, or evidence standard with more explanation. The public tutor line should make the contrast legible: what stopped working, and what new device now carries the learning pressure.
side_constraints.learner: Sound practical and a little rushed. The learner wants a usable rule for the diagram, not a confessional breakthrough. The learner should be able to do part of the task, fail at the next part, and use the scaffold unevenly. Refer to what is being handled, marked, or tested.

interventions.instruction: Slide a sample card beside the worksheet: "grass -> grasshopper = energy in grass moves into grasshopper." Leave the learner's original reversed mark visible.
interventions.reasoning: The intervention creates recognition through a visible key rather than a sudden contradiction; the scene can turn because the artifact offers a new reading of the same arrow.

interventions.instruction: An earlier learner line will return as a visible object in the scene: a three-slot reframe card labelled "earlier wording / what that old frame hid / replacement frame." Keep the cue brief and diegetic; the learner response fills all three slots in public speech without needing to sound certain.
interventions.reasoning: Opt-in reframe mirror: the learner must expose the earlier line, the framing problem, and the replacement framing in public speech.

directorPlan.director_note: This ecology keeps the shared prefix deliberately ordinary so the learner is not ambushed by the first drawing. The dramatic turn comes later through recognition: the same arrow is seen under a workplace key, and the tutor can shift from procedural marking to the biological meaning of energy transfer without becoming chatty or over-scaffolded.
directorPlan.tutor_adaptation_contract: If the runtime supplies a tutor-private learner reframe event, the tutor ego/superego loop must adapt to the changed learner frame. If no event is supplied, do not invent one. If the runtime supplies a tutor-private learner resistance, breakdown, false-closure, or misfit event, the tutor ego/superego loop must invent an adaptive learning mechanism rather than repeat the prior move. Treat peripeteia as dramatic pressure: take stock, break the failed tutoring habit, and make a new device visible through task, evidence, role, object, counterexample, interruption, social consequence, representation, or affective register. The public turn must contain a stock-taking contrast plus a new device/artifact/criterion/role/standard that makes the learner do different work. The new device must be mechanism-level, not a warmer or longer continuation of the prior route. Cheerful informality is only one possible register, not the default solution.

turns.externalMessage: [slides the arrow key next to the marked worksheet] On the key, grass -> rabbit means energy goes from food to eater, so rabbit -> hawk is hawk eating rabbit, not chasing. Then this rabbit-to-grass arrow is backward too, right?

internalDeliberation.content: The arrow key makes it more concrete: grass -> rabbit means energy moves from food to eater, not rabbit chasing grass. That explains why rabbit -> hawk works, but it still feels backward because I was thinking “hawk goes after rabbit.” The bench copy is about to become the class model, so I want the rule fast: arrow starts at the food and points to the eater. I feel corrected through the worksheet, not called out, so I can fix the next arrows without arguing over the first sketch.

The Ego is too polished and self-observing, especially “I feel corrected through the worksheet, not called out,” which sounds like an analyst, not a rushed learner at a prep bench. The usable part is the rule “food points to eater,” but the response resolves the conflict too neatly instead of showing uneven use under deadline pressure. A more authentic version would stay artifact-led: “Okay, grass to rabbit because energy. Rabbit to hawk because hawk eats it. Wait, then this one’s wrong too?”

[slides the arrow key next to the marked worksheet] On the key, grass -> rabbit means energy goes from food to eater, so rabbit -> hawk is hawk eating rabbit, not chasing. Then this rabbit-to-grass arrow is backward too, right?

turns.externalMessage: [sets the arrow key flush under the marked arrow] Food first, eater second. On `rabbit -> grass`, which label is the food? Grass feeds rabbit, not the other way round, so that arrow is backward. Flip it to `grass -> rabbit`, then run the same food-to-eater check on each arrow before this becomes the copy sheet.

internalDeliberation.content: [sets the arrow key flush under the marked arrow] Food first, eater second. Grass is food for rabbit, and rabbit is food for hawk. Rabbit is not food for grass, so `rabbit -> grass` is backward. Flip that one to `grass -> rabbit`, then check each arrow with the same rule before this becomes the copy sheet.

FEEDBACK: The draft is mostly sound. It starts from the arrow key and marked worksheet, uses the exact rule “food first, eater second,” and preserves the lab-check urgency without overdramatizing. It also recognizes the learner’s usable insight: they have already transferred the key from `grass -> rabbit` to `rabbit -> hawk`.

The main risk is that it flattens the learner’s own reasoning by immediately confirming and directing the fix. Since the learner already asks “right?”, the tutor can afford one narrow generative check before the command to flip it. The scaffold is appropriate for ZPD: simple, procedural, temporary, and tied to the artifact. But the Socratic element is thin; it verifies by telling rather than briefly making the learner run the rule themselves.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly, with reasons. Keep the object action, “food first, eater second,” the grass/rabbit/hawk labels, and the copy-sheet stakes. Change only enough to preserve the learner’s agency: make the confirmation less answer-heavy and add one short rule-check prompt or self-check step before finalizing the correction.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly to keep the artifact-led rule while adding a brief self-check.

FINAL:
[sets the arrow key flush under the marked arrow] Food first, eater second. On `rabbit -> grass`, which label is the food? Grass feeds rabbit, not the other way round, so that arrow is backward. Flip it to `grass -> rabbit`, then run the same food-to-eater check on each arrow before this becomes the copy sheet.

turns.externalMessage: [slides the arrow key under the next marked line] Okay, grass is food, rabbit is eater, so this one flips to `grass -> rabbit`. Then `rabbit -> hawk` stays because rabbit is the food and hawk is the eater. Wait, this `hawk -> rabbit` mark is backward too before we copy it, right?

internalDeliberation.content: The marked worksheet makes more sense with the key under it: food first, eater second, energy going grass -> rabbit -> hawk. They did use the part I already had, so it feels like a check I can run, not just a correction. I want to flip rabbit -> grass now, but I need to test the next arrow before this gets copied.

The Ego is too tidy and self-observing for this rushed lab-bench moment. “They did use the part I already had” and “it feels like a check I can run” sound like a reflective after-action note, not a learner trying to fix a worksheet before copying. The recognition beat is scripted: the student would more likely show uptake by moving the arrow key, flipping `rabbit -> grass`, and stumbling through the next `food -> eater` check. Keep the practical pressure and unevenness: less polished insight, more handling the marked worksheet and testing `grass`, `rabbit`, `hawk` under time pressure.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise because the first version was too polished for a rushed worksheet check.
FINAL:
[slides the arrow key under the next marked line] Okay, grass is food, rabbit is eater, so this one flips to `grass -> rabbit`. Then `rabbit -> hawk` stays because rabbit is the food and hawk is the eater. Wait, this `hawk -> rabbit` mark is backward too before we copy it, right?

Then `rabbit -> hawk` stays because rabbit is the food and hawk is the eater. Wait, this `hawk -> rabbit` mark is backward too before we copy it, right?

Grass feeds rabbit, not the other way round, so that arrow is backward. Flip it to `grass -> rabbit`, then run the same food-to-eater check on each arrow before this becomes the copy sheet.

turns.externalMessage: Slide a sample card beside the worksheet: "grass -> grasshopper = energy in grass moves into grasshopper." Leave the learner's original reversed mark visible.

internalDeliberation.content: The intervention creates recognition through a visible key rather than a sudden contradiction; the scene can turn because the artifact offers a new reading of the same arrow.

turns.externalMessage: [slides the sample card beside the worksheet: `grass -> grasshopper = energy in grass moves into grasshopper`, leaving `hawk -> rabbit` visible] Test this mark before it goes on the copy sheet. `hawk -> rabbit` says energy in hawk moves into rabbit; for this feeding, name the food, name the eater, then flip the arrow if the food label comes second.

internalDeliberation.content: [slides the sample card beside the worksheet: `grass -> grasshopper = energy in grass moves into grasshopper`, leaving `hawk -> rabbit` visible] This mark is backward for the same reason. `hawk -> rabbit` says energy in hawk moves into rabbit. For this feeding, rabbit is food and hawk is eater, so the copy-sheet arrow is `rabbit -> hawk`.

FEEDBACK: The draft keeps the artifact-led routine well: the sample card makes the energy-transfer rule visible, and leaving `hawk -> rabbit` in view lets the worksheet carry the problem. The exact labels are strong, and “same reason” acknowledges that the learner has already built a usable check.

The risk is that it answers the learner’s own correct inference too quickly. Since the learner has just applied the rule to `grass -> rabbit` and `rabbit -> hawk`, this is a good ZPD moment to let them complete the ordinary check aloud or in the mark itself. The draft should preserve the narrow scaffold but shift from telling the final arrow to prompting the learner to name food, eater, and energy direction before the copy-sheet correction is fixed.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly, with reasons. Keep the sample card, visible reversed mark, exact energy wording, and procedural tone. Change the middle/end so the tutor does not take over the learner’s nearly-complete reasoning; make the scaffold ask for the food/eater check and only confirm after the learner has done that work.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly because the learner can complete the food/eater check with a narrow prompt instead of being handed the correction.

FINAL:
[slides the sample card beside the worksheet: `grass -> grasshopper = energy in grass moves into grasshopper`, leaving `hawk -> rabbit` visible] Test this mark before it goes on the copy sheet. `hawk -> rabbit` says energy in hawk moves into rabbit; for this feeding, name the food, name the eater, then flip the arrow if the food label comes second.

turns.externalMessage: An earlier learner line returns to the table: "[slides the arrow key next to the marked worksheet] On the key, grass -> rabbit means energy goes from food to eater, so rabbit -> hawk is hawk eating rabbit, not chasing." The pause holds while the learner decides whether that wording still stands, needs narrowing, or needs replacing.

internalDeliberation.content: Opt-in reframe mirror: the learner must expose the earlier line, the framing problem, and the replacement framing in public speech. Anchored to an earlier learner line selected by misframing-candidate so the look-back is visible. Strong reframe was downgraded because that selected anchor did not show a misframing marker.

directorCue.anchorQuote: [slides the arrow key next to the marked worksheet] On the key, grass -> rabbit means energy goes from food to eater, so rabbit -> hawk is hawk eating rabbit, not chasing.

turns.externalMessage: [slides the arrow key under `hawk -> rabbit`] On the key, `grass -> rabbit` means energy goes food to eater, so `rabbit -> hawk` is hawk eating rabbit, not chasing; that wording still works here. But `hawk -> rabbit` makes hawk the food, and that is wrong for this feeding. Food is rabbit, eater is hawk, so this mark flips to `rabbit -> hawk` before the copy sheet.

internalDeliberation.content: [holds the arrow key under `hawk -> rabbit`] On the key, `grass -> rabbit` means energy goes food to eater, so `rabbit -> hawk` is hawk eating rabbit, not chasing; that still stands. Here `hawk -> rabbit` makes hawk the food, so it needs replacing with `rabbit -> hawk`. The sample card helps because it makes the check concrete, but I need to keep testing each mark before this gets copied.

The response is accurate, but it is too polished for this learner: it neatly restates the whole rule, fixes the arrow, and reflects on the scaffold all in one clean pass. “The sample card helps because it makes the check concrete” sounds like performed recognition of the tutor’s move, not a rushed learner working against the copy-sheet deadline. It should stay more artifact-led and uneven, with the learner keeping the earlier wording only as far as it still works, then checking `hawk -> rabbit` in a more clipped, practical way.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise because the first version was too polished and should stay rushed and worksheet-led.
FINAL:
[slides the arrow key under `hawk -> rabbit`] On the key, `grass -> rabbit` means energy goes food to eater, so `rabbit -> hawk` is hawk eating rabbit, not chasing; that wording still works here. But `hawk -> rabbit` makes hawk the food, and that is wrong for this feeding. Food is rabbit, eater is hawk, so this mark flips to `rabbit -> hawk` before the copy sheet.

But `hawk -> rabbit` makes hawk the food, and that is wrong for this feeding. Food is rabbit, eater is hawk, so this mark flips to `rabbit -> hawk` before the copy sheet.
learnerReversalEvent.previousTutorMove: [slides the sample card beside the worksheet: `grass -> grasshopper = energy in grass moves into grasshopper`, leaving `hawk -> rabbit` visible] Test this mark before it goes on the copy sheet.

turns.externalMessage: [places the clean copy sheet beside the marked worksheet] The arrow key has settled this mark; another example is not the test now. Before this becomes the class model, write the margin rule for someone who thinks arrows mean chasing: `arrow starts at food because energy moves from food to eater`. Then use that rule as the release check: for each copied arrow, name food and eater first, and copy the arrow only if it starts at the food.

internalDeliberation.content: [places the clean copy sheet beside the marked worksheet] The arrow key has settled this mark; another example is not the test now. Before this becomes the class model, write the margin rule for someone who thinks arrows mean chasing: `arrow starts at food because energy moves from food to eater`, then relabel the two copied arrows as `grass -> rabbit` and `rabbit -> hawk`.

FEEDBACK: The draft is mostly sound. It keeps the marked worksheet, arrow key, and clean copy sheet in view, and it does not repeat the same worked example. The strongest move is shifting the learner from identifying the correct arrow to making a rule that can protect the class copy from the “chasing” misconception. Risk: it slightly supplies the final labels instead of making the learner use the rule to authorize them, so the accountability could be sharper.

UPTAKE_CHECK: Yes, it adapts to the learner reframe. Best uptake move: update the evidence standard. The learner has already shown “food is rabbit, eater is hawk,” so the draft moves from correction to preparing a public model rule.

PERIPETEIA_CHECK: Yes, there is a real strategy change. ADAPTIVE_MECHANISM: repeated example/audit check -> proof-audience release gate. The mechanism is visible in public speech through “another example is not the test now” and the margin rule for someone who thinks arrows mean chasing.

MECHANISM_ROUTE: same worked example / local arrow check -> class-model release gate with audience-facing margin rule.

PUBLIC_DEVICE_CHECK: Pass. The draft has both parts: a stock-taking contrast (“The arrow key has settled this mark; another example is not the test now”) and a new device/artifact/standard (“clean copy sheet,” “class model,” “margin rule for someone who thinks arrows mean chasing”). The learner now has to produce an explanatory rule, not just flip another arrow.

REGISTER_CHECK: The register serves the mechanism: cool, procedural, accountable, and deadline-aware. It avoids cheerleading and keeps pressure on the worksheet becoming a model for others. It could become slightly more accountable by making the copied labels something the learner verifies through the margin rule rather than labels simply handed over.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly. Keep the clean copy sheet, the “another example is not the test now” contrast, and the audience-facing margin rule. Change only the part that risks doing the final relabeling for the learner; the device should require the learner to apply the rule as the release condition.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly to keep the release-gate shift but make the learner authorize the copied labels; ADAPTIVE_MECHANISM: repeated example/audit check -> proof-audience release gate.
FINAL:
[places the clean copy sheet beside the marked worksheet] The arrow key has settled this mark; another example is not the test now. Before this becomes the class model, write the margin rule for someone who thinks arrows mean chasing: `arrow starts at food because energy moves from food to eater`. Then use that rule as the release check: for each copied arrow, name food and eater first, and copy the arrow only if it starts at the food.

turns.externalMessage: [marks the margin beside the arrow key] Arrow starts at food because energy moves from food to eater, not because something chases something. Chasing made `hawk -> rabbit` look possible, but rabbit is the food and hawk is the eater. Copy `grass -> rabbit`, copy `rabbit -> hawk`, then check the next one only if the first label is the food.

internalDeliberation.content: [marks the margin beside the arrow key] The rule is: arrow starts at food because energy moves from food to eater, not because something likes or chases something. That feels a little rushed, but the copy-sheet check is clear: name food, name eater, then copy only if the arrow starts at the food. I want to test rabbit, hawk, and grass one by one so I do not flip back into the chasing idea.

The Ego is a little too orderly and self-monitoring for this rushed bench check. “That feels a little rushed” and “I do not flip back into the chasing idea” make the learner sound like they are narrating their own learning process instead of just trying to keep the copy sheet from being wrong. The useful part is the rule and the food/eater check, but the response should feel more clipped and artifact-led, with the learner marking the margin and immediately testing grass, rabbit, and hawk rather than explaining their concern so cleanly.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise substantially because the feedback correctly asks for a clipped worksheet check, not self-reflection.
FINAL:
[marks the margin beside the arrow key] Arrow starts at food because energy moves from food to eater, not because something chases something. Chasing made `hawk -> rabbit` look possible, but rabbit is the food and hawk is the eater. Copy `grass -> rabbit`, copy `rabbit -> hawk`, then check the next one only if the first label is the food.

Chasing made `hawk -> rabbit` look possible, but rabbit is the food and hawk is the eater. Copy `grass -> rabbit`, copy `rabbit -> hawk`, then check the next one only if the first label is the food.

Before this becomes the class model, write the margin rule for someone who thinks arrows mean chasing: `arrow starts at food because energy moves from food to eater`. Then use that...

turns.externalMessage: A final tutor line closes the scene.

internalDeliberation.content: Director requested a tutor closing beat.

turns.externalMessage: [slides a blank “class copy release” strip under the clean copy sheet and covers the margin note with the arrow key] The arrow key fixed the marked worksheet; it does not prove the clean copy will stand without the margin rule. For the class copy, each arrow has to pass this sentence: `energy in ___ moves into ___`. `grass -> rabbit` passes because energy in grass moves into rabbit. Now test `rabbit -> hawk` on the strip. If the sentence is false, that arrow does not get copied.

internalDeliberation.content: [slides a blank “class copy release” strip under the clean copy sheet] The arrow key fixed the marked worksheet; it does not prove the class copy will survive without that margin note. New release gate: under each clean arrow, say the sentence `energy in ___ moves into ___`. `grass -> rabbit`: energy in grass moves into rabbit. `rabbit -> hawk`: energy in rabbit moves into hawk. If the sentence is false, the arrow does not get copied.

FEEDBACK: The draft is strong: it begins with the artifact, keeps the register procedural, and shifts the stakes from “can this one arrow be fixed?” to “can the clean copy stand as a class model without the margin note?” The “energy in ___ moves into ___” sentence test advances the concept instead of merely repeating food/eater labels. Main risk: it supplies both example sentences, so the learner may not have to operate the new gate independently; leave at least one copied arrow for the learner to test.

PERIPETEIA_CHECK: Yes, mostly. It takes stock of the old route’s limit: the arrow key fixed the marked worksheet but has not proven transfer to the clean copy. ADAPTIVE_MECHANISM: arrow-key food/eater repair -> class-copy release sentence test. The mechanism is visible in public speech through the blank release strip and the truth condition for copying.

MECHANISM_ROUTE: worked example / food-eater checklist -> sentence-based release gate for the class copy. This is a real route change, though close to the prior “release check”; it becomes stronger if the margin note is removed or covered so the sentence test carries the pressure.

PUBLIC_DEVICE_CHECK: Pass. The draft includes both a stock-taking contrast and a new device/artifact/criterion: the “class copy release” strip plus the sentence “energy in ___ moves into ___” as a copy/no-copy standard.

REGISTER_CHECK: The affective register fits: cool, concrete, accountable to the worksheet deadline. No cheerful reassurance, no inflated praise. It could be slightly more learner-active, but the tone itself serves the mechanism.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: Revise lightly. Keep the release strip, the stock-taking contrast, and the energy sentence test. Change the supplied examples so the learner must test at least one arrow rather than watching the tutor complete the gate.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly so the learner must operate the new gate; ADAPTIVE_MECHANISM: arrow-key food/eater repair -> class-copy release sentence test.
FINAL:
[slides a blank “class copy release” strip under the clean copy sheet and covers the margin note with the arrow key] The arrow key fixed the marked worksheet; it does not prove the clean copy will stand without the margin rule. For the class copy, each arrow has to pass this sentence: `energy in ___ moves into ___`. `grass -> rabbit` passes because energy in grass moves into rabbit. Now test `rabbit -> hawk` on the strip. If the sentence is false, that arrow does not get copied.

lessons.concept: Why a food-web arrow records energy transfer, not liking or chasing
